title: Sensors for 3D Vision
concept: 3D detectors utilize LiDAR point clouds, depth cameras, or stereo disparity to capture physical spatial dimensions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which rotation angle represents a vehicle's steering heading on flat ground?
exercises.explain: Correct! Yaw is rotation around the vertical height axis, matching steering heading.

cards.title: How Computers Feel Depth
cards.body: How does a computer actually measure depth? Standard camera sensors only capture flat 2D grids of red, green, and blue light. What special hardware sensors do AI systems use to see the third dimension?

cards.title: 3D Sensors
cards.body: 3D detectors rely on specialized sensors: LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging, which shoots lasers to create a 3D point cloud), Depth Cameras (RGB-D, which measure infrared reflection), and Stereo Cameras (which calculate depth using two offset lenses).

cards.title: Echolocation vs. Binocular Vision
cards.body: LiDAR is like a bat using echolocation: it shoots laser pulses (sound waves) and times their return to build a map of physical space. Stereo vision is like human eyes: your brain compares the slight offset between what your left and right eyes see to calculate depth.

cards.title: LiDAR Point Clouds
cards.body: A self-driving car roof has a spinning LiDAR. It shoots 1 million laser pulses per second. By measuring the flight time of each laser reflection, it creates a point cloud — a dense set of millions of 3D coordinates representing surrounding shapes.

cards.title: Is LiDAR Mandatory?
cards.body: You might think 3D detection requires expensive LiDAR. Fact: While LiDAR is the most accurate, modern monocular and stereo vision algorithms can predict 3D boxes from standard camera feeds alone, lowering system cost.

cards.title: Sensor Outputs compared
cards.body: How different sensors represent the surrounding space:

left.text:
Outputs color pixels with depth values
High resolution, rich textures
Range limited, sensitive to lighting

right.text:
Outputs 3D point cloud coordinates
Highly accurate distances up to 200m
No texture details, sparse data

exercises.prompt: What is a 'point cloud' in 3D computer vision?

options.text: A dense set of 3D coordinate points generated by LiDAR representing physical surfaces
A cloud storage database for saving images
A blur effect applied to the edges of bounding boxes
A collection of 2D pixel coordinates with color values

exercises.explain: Correct! A point cloud is a collection of 3D points representing the surfaces of surrounding objects.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Stereo cameras calculate depth by comparing the slight horizontal differences (disparity) between two offset camera lenses.
exercises.explain: True. Disparity between two lenses behaves like human binocular vision to calculate depth.

exercises.sentence: Autonomous vehicles often use a laser-ranging sensor called ___ to scan the environment in 3D.
exercises.explain: LiDAR shoots laser pulses to measure precise 3D distances.

exercises.prompt: What is a main disadvantage of LiDAR sensors compared to cameras?

options.text: They are much more expensive and do not capture color or texture details
They only work in pitch-black darkness

exercises.explain: Correct! LiDAR provides precise 3D coordinates but lacks color and texture information, and is historically expensive.

exercises.prompt: You are building a vacuum robot to work indoors in varying light conditions. You need to map furniture outlines cheaply. Which sensor setup is best?

options.text: An RGB-D depth camera, which uses active infrared projection to capture depth maps cheaply and works in the dark.
A spinning 64-channel server-grade LiDAR on top of the vacuum.
A single cheap monocular camera with no depth features.

exercises.explain: Exactly! RGB-D depth cameras are cheap, compact, and perfect for indoor robotic depth sensing.
